articles.title: Canyon Partners Real Estate Adds Four Executives to Investment Platform
articles.narrative: Canyon Partners Real Estate has expanded its executive team with four key appointments, including Scott Swisher as head of Western region credit originations, Adam Ostrowsky as managing director of capital markets, Scott San Filippo as senior director of bridge debt, and Spencer Schlee as head of opportunistic equity. These strategic hires and promotions are intended to strengthen the firm's real estate platform and capitalize on opportunities in both lending and opportunistic investing, reflecting an ongoing evolution of the firm's capabilities.

people.name: Scott Swisher
people.title: Managing Director and Head of Western Region Credit Originations

people.name: Spencer Schlee
people.title: Managing Director and Head of Opportunistic Equity

articles.title: CMBS Distress Rises in 17 of the 25 Largest U.S. Markets
articles.narrative: A May 2026 analysis of commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) distress by CRED iQ reveals a national distress rate of 12.7 percent across the 25 largest U.S. metropolitan statistical areas, an increase from 12.2 percent in June 2025. Seventeen of these markets experienced year-over-year increases, with Minneapolis, Denver, and Rochester, N.Y., showing the highest distress rates. The most significant escalations were observed in St. Louis, Oklahoma City, and Pittsburgh, largely due to concentrated office and mixed-use loan exposure. Conversely, Providence, Charlotte, and Austin saw improvements in their distress rates, reflecting successful loan resolutions and stronger market dynamics in some Sun Belt areas.

articles.title: Hey, Retailers, the Soccer Ball’s in Your Court This World Cup
articles.narrative: This article argues that the FIFA World Cup presents a significant opportunity for retailers, restaurateurs, and U.S. downtowns to attract foot traffic and new customers. The author emphasizes that this is not a passive opportunity, but requires intentional strategies focused on fan behavior before and after games, such as extending hours, offering specials, and implementing outdoor activations. Partnerships between businesses and downtown organizations, like those seen in New York City, are highlighted as crucial for maximizing economic impact by spreading visitor movement beyond single anchor destinations. The piece also suggests tactics like country-themed menus, viewing parties, limited-time merchandise, and pop-up shops, while stressing the importance of data analysis for staffing and security.

articles.title: Realterm CEO Bob Fordi Is Transforming the IOS Business
articles.narrative: Realterm, a multinational transportation and logistics real estate investment firm, has experienced significant growth under CEO Bob Fordi, tripling its assets from $4.5 billion to $14 billion since 2019. The firm, a pioneer in industrial outdoor storage (IOS) and a leader in air cargo facilities, has expanded its global footprint to include Amsterdam, Singapore, Australia, and soon, Japan. Realterm's strategy focuses on acquiring and developing 'high flow-through' properties designed to move freight efficiently, establishing a foundational grip on critical transportation logistics assets.

people.title: Managing Director and Global Head of Capital Markets

articles.title: Data Centers Are Plugging a Big Hole in the U.S. Construction Industry
articles.narrative: The U.S. construction industry is experiencing a significant shift, with data center and advanced manufacturing projects increasingly dominating overall activity, while multifamily construction faces substantial headwinds. This disparity is so pronounced that excluding data center and manufacturing work would reveal a flat or declining pace of construction nationally. Factors such as high interest rates, elevated material and labor costs, and a slowdown in population and employment growth are hindering multifamily development, leading to a shrinking pipeline of new projects and impacting contractor revenues. While some markets like South Florida continue to see multifamily activity, the national outlook suggests ongoing challenges for financing and delivering new rental housing units.

people.name: Sonny Maken
people.title: Chief Operating Officer of the South Florida chapter

articles.title: Core Spaces to develop three new student housing developments near University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign
articles.narrative: Core Spaces, a prominent student housing developer, has announced plans for three new purpose-built student housing developments near the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. These projects are expected to deliver over 3,500 beds between 2028 and 2031, significantly expanding the firm's presence in the market. The expansion builds on Core Spaces' existing portfolio in Champaign, which includes previous developments and recent acquisitions along Green Street.

data_points.project_notes: This project is the first of three new developments, with the remaining two planned to deliver between 2028 and 2031, totaling over 3,500 beds across all three projects.

articles.title: Retail sector keeps showing off its resilience
articles.narrative: Retail sales demonstrated continued resilience in April, with overall sales rising 4.6% year-over-year and foot traffic increasing by 2.6%, according to Colliers' latest report. This indicates that consumers are still engaging with brick-and-mortar stores despite economic uncertainties. Discretionary spending, particularly in apparel and electronics, saw significant boosts, with electronics sales jumping 9.1% and clothing store traffic rising nearly 4%. Value-oriented retailers also continued to perform strongly, attracting budget-conscious shoppers, while housing-related retail segments and experiential categories showed signs of cooling.

articles.title: PCCP, The Dinerstein Companies acquire student housing communities in College Station
articles.narrative: A joint venture between PCCP and The Dinerstein Companies has acquired two adjacent student housing communities in College Station, TX, totaling 1,128 beds. The properties, formerly known as 2818 Apartments and Parkway Place, have been rebranded as Sterling College Station. The new owners plan a comprehensive renovation and operational improvement program, including upgraded unit interiors, fitness center improvements, and new pickleball courts, to enhance the community's appeal to Texas A&M University students.

data_points.address: 1300 Harvey Mitchell Parkway South, College Station, TX
data_points.notable_features: Low-density cottage- and garden-style communities offering larger layouts and expansive indoor and outdoor amenity spaces, served by the AggieSpirit bus system.
data_points.project_notes: Properties delivered in 2008 and 2009. The joint venture plans to combine operations into a single community and implement in-house property management.

articles.title: The Can Gets Heavier: Texas multifamily hopes for shift in capital
articles.narrative: Multifamily owners in Texas are facing increased pressure to sell or refinance assets as capital markets continue to favor a "higher for longer" interest rate environment, despite earlier hopes for rate cuts. While operating fundamentals are showing uneven improvement across submarkets, refinancing challenges are primarily driving transaction activity, with many sales involving smoothly operating properties burdened by distressed capital stacks rather than operational distress. The market is characterized by significant submarket divergence, with some areas experiencing rent growth and strong absorption, while others, particularly in North Dallas, continue to offer substantial concessions. Buyers are increasingly focused on yield-on-cost frameworks and current asset production rather than theoretical stabilization values, with assumable low-rate debt emerging as a key selling point for properties.

articles.market_intelligence: Austin reported its first quarter of rent growth since Q2 2023. San Antonio's annual absorption has outpaced deliveries for two straight years. San Antonio cap rates are running approximately 25 basis points higher than Austin. North Dallas, which accounted for roughly half of DFW units under construction, has six to eight weeks of free rent quoted as standard across northern Collin County and North Fort Worth.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap brokers sale of 7,295-square-foot retail property in Anna
articles.narrative: A Dallas-area private partnership has acquired a 7,295-square-foot multi-tenant retail property in Anna, Texas, as part of a 1031 exchange. The all-cash transaction closed in less than 45 days, with the buyer strategically reallocating equity from an alternative property type into triple-net retail assets within high-growth DFW submarkets. The acquisition was part of a two-property portfolio that included an adjacent shopping center.

data_points.notable_features: Shadow-anchored by a Walmart, located in a fast-growing suburban corridor.
data_points.project_notes: Acquisition was part of a 1031 exchange and included an adjacent shopping center.

articles.title: AI is creating new challenges, opportunities and questions about the industry’s future
articles.narrative: This article explores the dual impact of artificial intelligence on the commercial real estate industry, highlighting both new opportunities and significant challenges. AI companies are driving substantial office leasing demand in major tech hubs like San Francisco and New York, and fueling a boom in data center development, particularly in Texas. However, the technology also raises concerns about potential long-term reductions in office-based jobs, which could diminish future space needs. Additionally, AI is influencing residential real estate by providing buyers and sellers with market analysis, while also requiring agents to refine their expertise to address AI-generated insights and maintain the human element in transactions.

articles.title: Developer seizes on affordable, workforce market with condos, townhomes
articles.narrative: Prospera Real Estate Collective, led by Ellen Buckley, is developing two affordable and workforce housing projects in Miami-Dade County, Florida. The projects include Grand Bahamas Place, a 56-unit condo building in Coconut Grove, and Faith Place Village, a 37-unit townhome community near North Miami. Grand Bahamas Place will offer units restricted to households earning up to 80 percent of the area median income, while Faith Place Village will cater to households earning up to 120 percent of the AMI. These developments address South Florida's critical need for affordable and workforce housing, with Prospera partnering with local churches and community groups to unlock value from underutilized land.

data_points.notable_features: Condos will range from studios to two bedrooms and are restricted for households earning up to 80 percent of the area median income. The project includes nearly 3,000 square feet of retail and a 4,000-square-foot religious and community space.
data_points.project_notes: The partnership paid $2.2 million for one lot in 2025, and Believers of Authority Ministries transferred the other lot for $100 in 2025. Condo prices will range from $305,000 to $450,000. The project is named in homage to the area's earliest settlers from the Bahamas.

articles.financing: Grand Bahamas Place received $4.2 million in Miami Forever bond financing and $700,000 from Florida Community Loan Fund. Prospera is working to obtain a construction loan for both projects.

articles.title: South Florida’s top deals: Miami Beach estate sells for $24M
articles.narrative: An affiliate of Link Logistics sold an industrial property in Fort Lauderdale for $17.2 million to an LLC tied to Dalfen International. The transaction for the 66,000-square-foot property equates to approximately $260 per square foot. This deal represents the top commercial transaction in South Florida for the period.

data_points.project_notes: Seller acquired the property in 2017 for $6.1 million.

articles.market_intelligence: CRE deal activity in April saw a 33% year-over-year decline to $24.7 billion, but year-to-date volume reached $163.5 billion, marking a 14% increase compared to the previous year, suggesting overall market stability.

articles.title: South Florida Dirt: Miami-Dade County vs. Fisher Island’s billionaires
articles.narrative: Miami-Dade County has abandoned its proposed $400 million deal to acquire a nearly 10-acre fuel depot on Fisher Island from HRP Group, opting instead to pursue the property through eminent domain. HRP Group had previously acquired the site for $180 million in October, with plans to demolish the fuel facility, remediate the land, and develop luxury condominiums, conveying 4 acres to the Fisher Island Community Association. The county's decision to pursue eminent domain comes after a lawsuit filed by residents against HRP and the resignation of two county officials, highlighting a contentious battle over the site's future use and the county's need for continued fuel supply for PortMiami cruise ships.

data_points.notable_features: Aging fuel depot critical for PortMiami cruise ship fuel supply; located on Fisher Island, an exclusive community accessible only by ferry, boat, or helicopter.
data_points.project_notes: HRP Group acquired the property for $180 million in October, with TransMontaigne Partners continuing operations until 2027. Miami-Dade County initially proposed a $400 million purchase ($200 million at closing, $200 million over 20 years) before abandoning the deal for eminent domain.

articles.title: Movers: Five-person Avison Young industrial team jumps to JLL
articles.narrative: A five-person industrial brokerage team, including Managing Director Adam Haefner, Executive Vice Presidents Marty Mikaitis and Zeke Rowan, Vice President Nick Fazio, and Sales Coordinator Anne McGrath, has moved from Avison Young to JLL in Chicago. The team will focus on industrial clients in the O'Hare submarket, bringing a combined deal volume of over $2.5 billion across 50 million square feet. Additionally, Core Industrial Realty hired Alexander "AJ" Smith as a new broker, Lee & Associates promoted Tony Russo to Executive Vice President and Matthew Androwich to Vice President, and McHugh Construction added Eric Engstrom as Project Executive, Grant Peoples as Director of Risk Management, and Amit Patel as Project Manager. Jordan Multak also joined Greenstone Partners, specializing in multifamily and mixed-use sectors.

articles.title: One-two-three punch of Mavs, Stars and Neiman bruises struggling Downtown Dallas
articles.narrative: Downtown Dallas is facing significant challenges with the departure of several key anchors. The Dallas Mavericks are relocating to Valley View, the Dallas Stars are leaving American Airlines Center, and the Neiman Marcus flagship store at 1618 Main Street is closing in September due to Saks Global's bankruptcy. These departures follow AT&T's earlier decision to move its 2 million-square-foot office footprint to Plano, further exacerbating the urban core's struggles. Meanwhile, Plano continues to attract major businesses, including Samsung's relocation of its U.S. operations from New Jersey.
